company.name: Tapestry
company.description: Tapestry owns Coach and Kate Spade New York, global fashion and accessories brands focused on handbags, leather goods, apparel, footwear, and lifestyle products.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Tapestry is a New York-based fashion group whose current consumer brand portfolio is Coach and Kate Spade New York after completing the sale of Stuart Weitzman in 2025; Coach was founded in 1941 and Kate Spade New York in 1993.

Coach is the scale engine: the fiscal 2025 Form 10-K reported $5.60 billion of Coach net sales, about 79.9% of company net sales, while Kate Spade generated $1.20 billion after a year-over-year decline.

overview.title: Recent Performance
paragraphs: Fiscal 2025 net sales were $7.01 billion, operating income was $415.0 million, and net income was $183.2 million after large Kate Spade impairment charges.

In the fiscal third quarter ended March 28, 2026, reported coverage showed Tapestry revenue up 19% on a constant-currency basis to $1.9 billion, with Coach up 29% and Kate Spade down 11%, reinforcing the performance gap between the two brands.

moatNarrative: Tapestry's moat is brand-led rather than technical. Coach has long heritage, strong handbag recognition, global stores and e-commerce, and enough marketing scale to keep acquiring younger customers.

Kate Spade weakens the portfolio narrative because it has been in a reset with declining sales, but the combined direct-to-consumer network, supplier base, design calendar, and merchandising data still create operational advantages that local makers cannot copy quickly.
decentralizationNarrative: The underlying goods are decentralizable: handbags, wallets, apparel, and accessories can be designed, patterned, repaired, and manufactured by small workshops using open pattern tools and local materials.

The harder pieces to decentralize are status, taste-making, quality assurance, returns, warranty handling, and supply-chain trust. Open pattern commons, repair and resale networks, and maker cooperatives can pressure generic products and repair demand, but they do not fully replace a global fashion brand.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 276-300; rankApprox uses the midpoint of that cohort until a canonical exact rank is available. Snapshot refreshed on 2026-07-11 using fiscal 2025 Form 10-K figures, brand pages, Q3 FY2026 coverage, CompaniesMarketCap market data, and open pattern and supply-chain sources.

moat.rationale: Coach represented 79.9% of fiscal 2025 net sales, grew nearly 10% for the year, and continued strong fiscal 2026 quarterly growth; Kate Spade weakness keeps the score below a top-tier luxury moat.

decentralizability.rationale: Physical goods and patterning can move to local makers, repair shops, resale networks, and open design tools, but branded aspiration, quality control, and global retail and marketing reduce full decentralizability.

profitability.rationale: Fiscal 2025 GAAP profit was depressed by impairment charges, but the company still generated $415.0 million of operating income and showed strong fiscal 2026 revenue and margin momentum led by Coach.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported a trailing P/E ratio of about 58.5 as of June 2026; the multiple is distorted by depressed trailing earnings after fiscal 2025 impairment charges.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Tapestry's market capitalization at about $29.49 billion as of June 2026; the registry uses a rounded value because market capitalization changes continuously.

products.name: Coach
products.summary: Coach is Tapestry's flagship global fashion brand, centered on handbags, leather goods, accessories, footwear, and lifestyle collections.
products.whyItMatters: Coach drives most of Tapestry's sales and brand value, making it the main test of whether open design, local production, repair, and resale can pressure accessible luxury accessories.
replacementSketch: A near-term replacement would not clone Coach's brand. It would turn open pattern files, local leatherworkers, repair shops, and resale inventory into a visible catalog of durable bags and accessories.
Consumers would trade some brand cachet for made-to-measure utility, repairability, provenance, and local service.

alternatives.name: Seamly2D
alternatives.summary: Seamly2D is GPLv3+ open-source patternmaking software for custom-fit garment and accessory pattern files, available across major desktop platforms.

openness.rationale: The repository describes Seamly2D as open-source patternmaking software released under the GPLv3+ license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Patternmaking software lets independent designers and makers create custom files without depending on a brand-owned design system.

readiness.rationale: The project lists Windows, macOS, and Linux availability and showed recent 2026 releases, but it is a design tool rather than a complete retail or manufacturing stack.

costLeverage.rationale: Open pattern software can reduce design and tooling costs for small makers, while materials, skilled labor, finishing, hardware, and fulfillment remain expensive.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open-Pattern Leather Goods Microfactories
disruptionConcepts.summary: Local leatherworkers and small workshops could use open pattern files, shared quality standards, repair logs, and local material sourcing to produce durable bags and accessories outside a brand-owned product calendar.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Coach's centralized fashion calendar and brand premium face pressure if useful bag patterns, repair workflows, and local maker reputation become easier to coordinate than buying another branded seasonal product.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralized manufacturing is the core mechanism. Bitcoin is not required; the important shift is from a single brand controlling design, production, and marketing toward many local operators using open patterns and shared standards.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Designers publish pattern files and material notes, workshops list capacity and sample work, buyers commission or purchase locally, and cooperatives maintain shared grading, warranty, and repair rules.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Pattern hashes, material receipts, maker identity, photo records, repair histories, local pickup inspection, and delayed settlement can constrain fake fulfillment. Reputation loss and cooperative expulsion punish repeated misgrading, but subjective quality remains difficult to verify fully.
failureModes: Maker quality and finishing may not match Coach's brand-controlled factories, especially for leather stitching, hardware, and returns.
Demand may remain niche because many buyers are purchasing status, campaign imagery, and resale-recognized brand identity rather than only product utility.
adoptionPath: Start with repairs, straps, wallets, organizers, and simple bags where local inspection and customization matter.
Expand into cooperative catalogs once makers can prove consistent finishing, warranty service, and material disclosure.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept distributes design, fabrication, repair, and service across local workshops rather than one brand-controlled chain.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open pattern tools and supply-chain disclosure primitives exist, but maker marketplaces need hard quality, warranty, and dispute rules.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small-batch leather goods are feasible, but consistent luxury-grade finishing, hardware sourcing, and consumer service are hard to standardize.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The model can pressure repair, customization, and generic accessory demand, but Coach's brand heat and global marketing remain strong defenses.

disruptionConcepts.name: Authenticated Resale and Repair Commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: Independent repair shops, consignors, and local pickup points could coordinate around portable authenticity records, condition grades, and repair histories so existing Coach-class bags circulate longer without relying on a brand-controlled secondary market.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: This pressures Coach less by inventing a rival brand and more by reducing repurchase frequency: durable bags stay in service through trusted resale, repair, and provenance rather than seasonal replacement.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation and peer-to-peer marketplace coordination are central. Bitcoin or Lightning could settle low-fee cross-border payouts for repair or escrow, but the core mechanism is portable reputation and inventory records rather than a payment rail.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Sellers list bags with photos and material notes, local authenticators or repairers attach condition and repair attestations, buyers search nearby inventory, and federated storefronts share reputation without one platform owning every listing.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Authenticity checks, serial or hardware photos, repair logs, escrow or delayed settlement, and pickup inspection constrain counterfeit listings and fake condition claims; repeated fraud reduces listing privileges across participating nodes.
failureModes: Counterfeit handbags and disputed authenticity judgments can destroy trust faster than the network can recover.
Many buyers still prefer new branded product for status and warranty, limiting how much resale and repair displace primary sales.
adoptionPath: Start with city-level repair and consignment partners that already handle leather goods and accessories.
Add interoperable condition schemas, portable seller reputation, and optional escrow once local inventory density and dispute handling are stable.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Ownership, authentication, repair, and fulfillment can be distributed across many local operators instead of one brand marketplace.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Resale and repair already exist commercially; the harder part is portable trust and interoperable catalogs rather than inventing the behavior.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: A city pilot is feasible with existing repair and consignment operators, while scalable authenticity standards remain the main execution risk.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Trusted resale and repair can slow repurchase cycles for durable handbags, but Coach still benefits from newness, marketing, and brand-controlled retail.

products.name: Kate Spade New York
products.category: fashion accessories, apparel, and lifestyle retail
products.summary: Kate Spade New York is Tapestry's colorful lifestyle brand spanning handbags, wallets, jewelry, shoes, clothing, accessories, home decor, and gifts.
products.whyItMatters: Kate Spade is strategically important but weaker than Coach, making it a clearer candidate for pressure from open design, made-to-measure apparel, local makers, resale, and community-led fashion identity.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement would combine open pattern generation, local sewists, community boutiques, and small-batch accessory makers rather than trying to build another centralized brand.
The replacement would win first on fit, customization, repair, provenance, and lower platform rent, not on immediate parity with Kate Spade's marketing reach.

alternatives.name: FreeSewing
alternatives.summary: FreeSewing is open-source software for generating bespoke sewing patterns and sharing made-to-measure designs with a community.

openness.rationale: FreeSewing describes itself as open-source software, and its public repository is available under an MIT license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The project supports individual measurement sets and bespoke pattern generation, which fits local and self-directed clothing production.

readiness.rationale: FreeSewing is usable and community-facing, but it is a volunteer pattern platform rather than a complete commerce, manufacturing, or fulfillment network.

costLeverage.rationale: Open bespoke patterns can reduce design and fit costs for home sewers and small makers, though fabric, labor, finishing, and customer acquisition still dominate final economics.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated Slow-Fashion Pattern Commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: Independent designers, sewists, and small boutiques could coordinate around open pattern files, shared measurement tools, and local production nodes to offer colorful made-to-measure clothing and accessories without centralizing brand ownership.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Kate Spade's lifestyle positioning is vulnerable where consumers care more about distinctiveness, fit, and local identity than a central brand mark.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation and cooperative production are central. Bitcoin or Lightning could be added for small cross-border maker payouts, but the core mechanism is interoperable pattern, reputation, and order coordination.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Designers publish pattern modules, local makers accept orders or host workshops, federated storefronts share reputation and availability, and buyers choose between home production, local tailoring, or cooperative fulfillment.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Pattern hashes, sample photos, material disclosures, maker reviews, escrow or delayed settlement, fitting confirmations, and local pickup inspection constrain fake fulfillment and misrepresented quality.
failureModes: The network may struggle to create a coherent aesthetic without becoming another centralized brand curator.
Low-volume sewing and accessory production may cost more than mass retail, limiting appeal to buyers who prioritize price or brand recognition.
adoptionPath: Begin with made-to-measure dresses, skirts, small accessories, and repair or alteration services for local communities.
Federate catalogs and reputation once makers prove repeatable fit, delivery times, and customer service.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept distributes design, production, storefront operation, and customer relationships across many independent makers and communities.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open pattern tools make coordination plausible, but shared sizing, order routing, dispute handling, and aesthetic curation are still operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small-scale made-to-measure production is feasible with existing tools, but scaling consistent delivery and returns is difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The model can pressure niche apparel and customization demand, but Kate Spade's brand, stores, marketing, and accessory assortment remain significant advantages.

disruptionConcepts.name: Local Textile Scrap Remanufacturing Loop
disruptionConcepts.summary: Neighborhood workshops and cooperatives could recover textile scraps, deadstock, returned accessories, and worn garments into remade bags, small goods, and lifestyle products using shared material grades and open pattern modules.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: This pressures Kate Spade's novelty-driven lifestyle assortment by making colorful accessories and apparel from local material streams instead of continuous brand-new seasonal inventory.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Local materials processing and cooperative production are the core decentralization mechanisms. Bitcoin is not required; value shifts toward shared scrap grading, remanufacturing capacity, and community ownership of inventory loops.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Collectors and thrift partners deliver sorted textiles, cooperatives grade and process materials, makers remanufacture goods from open patterns, and local storefronts or markets sell finished pieces with material provenance.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Material batch IDs, fiber disclosures, before-and-after photos, maker identity, and local pickup inspection constrain mislabeled remade goods; cooperatives can refuse suppliers that repeatedly misgrade scrap or contaminate batches.
failureModes: Sorting, cleaning, and finishing recovered textiles can cost more than buying new commodity inputs.
Remade aesthetics may not satisfy buyers who want polished brand-new presentation and predictable sizing.
adoptionPath: Pilot with thrift partners, alteration shops, and scrap collectors focused on small accessories and repaired apparel.
Expand into remanufactured bags and gift goods once material grading, hygiene handling, and consistent finishing are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Material recovery, remanufacturing, and sales can be run by many local operators rather than a single branded supply chain.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open patterns and supplier-transparency primitives help, but scrap logistics and quality grading remain operationally heavy.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Small remake pilots are feasible today, while consistent materials hygiene, hardware recovery, and finished-goods quality are still hard.

incumbentPressure.rationale: A remanufacturing loop can pressure niche gift and lifestyle demand, but Kate Spade retains marketing, assortment breadth, and new-product appeal.
